featured.category: Ali Reviews · Shopify · UX Writing · Conversion
featured.title: Modal $1: When Transparency Beats Marketing
featured.excerpt: We ran an A/B test on Ali Reviews' $1 pricing modal — a transparent approach vs a marketing-heavy one. The data was clear: hiding the free plan doubled uninstall rates. Transparency won, and so did our users.

modal.context:
Ali Reviews wanted more users to continue with a $1 first-month offer when they selected the free plan. The goal: convert free users into paid users by letting them try premium features at very low risk. My role as UX Writer was to write both versions of the pricing modal and shape the copy strategy for the experiment.

Team: Product Manager, UX Designer, Engineer, Quality Control. Tools: Figma, Atlassian.
modal.problem: Same-day uninstalls were already a major problem. While the business wanted to increase conversions, we couldn't afford to make users feel tricked. The challenge: write copy that encourages the $1 upgrade without eroding trust — because a user who uninstalls the same day is worse than one who stays on the free plan.
modal.approach:
We designed two versions of the pricing modal and ran an A/B test with new users between April 1–14.

Version A (Transparent): Clearly showed both Free Plan ($0) and AI Features Plan ($1/first month, then $14.95/month). Users could compare and choose freely. CTAs: "Keep Basic plan" vs "Try AI features for free." Hypothesis: transparency builds trust and reduces uninstalls.

Version B (Marketing-Heavy): Free plan was less visible. Stronger promotional headline — "Unlock Super 14 Days Free, Then Enjoy Your First Month for Just $1!" CTAs: "Maybe later" vs "Grab the deal." Hypothesis: hiding the free plan would push users toward the paid tier by default.
modal.delivered: Two fully written and designed pricing modal variants, a copy rationale document for each version, and a post-test analysis with recommendations for future pricing copy across both Ali Reviews and TrueProfit.
modal.impact:
Version A: 300 users chose the Super Plan. Version B: 251 users chose the Super Plan.

But the real story was uninstalls: Version A had ~18% same-day uninstalls. Version B had ~36% — nearly double. Users who couldn't easily find the free plan felt trapped and left immediately.

We kept Version A. Key takeaway: when it comes to pricing copy, transparency isn't just the ethical choice — it's the better business decision. Users who feel respected stay longer and pay eventually.

cases.title: TrueProfit: Tone & Voice System
cases.description: Defining how TrueProfit speaks across the entire product — from mission alignment to brand personality workshops to a final tone framework used across all in-app content.

modal.context:
As part of the TrueProfit product team, I collaborated on multiple workshops with the content team to define the app's tone and voice branding. We discussed our mission and vision, shared individual reflections, and aligned on different tone ranges before reaching a final framework.

This process not only helped unify the product's communication style but also ensured that every message reflects the brand's values consistently. The outcome was a clear and actionable tone and voice guide that can be applied across in-app content, support messages, and marketing materials.
modal.problem: TrueProfit had grown fast. Different features had been written at different times by different people. The result: a product that didn't sound like itself. Some screens were warm and conversational. Others were cold and technical. Merchants felt like they were moving between different apps — and no one had a shared reference for what TrueProfit should sound like.
modal.approach:
We started with mission and vision alignment. TrueProfit's vision — an eCommerce world where merchants drive towards profit, not just revenue — became the north star for every tone decision.

Next, each team member individually sorted personality words into three buckets: Who We Are, Who We'd Like to Be, and Who We're Not. The exercise surfaced both shared instincts and useful tensions in how the team saw the brand.

Through open discussion, we converged on five brand personality words: Straightforward, Neat, Trustworthy, Expert, Pioneer.

Finally, we mapped individual tone ranges across five spectrums — Clear↔Blunt, Organized↔Flawless, Reliable↔Unquestionable, Skilled↔Masterful, Forward-Thinking↔Revolutionary — and debated where TrueProfit sits on each. The final positions became the guardrails for all copy decisions going forward.

ab_images.label: Brand Personality Exercise — Who We Are · Who We'd Like to Be · Who We're Not

modal.delivered: A living Tone & Voice guideline document for the entire product team — covering brand personality, writing principles, tone by context (success / warning / error / empty states), and annotated before/after examples. Documented and shared across the Figma design system. New features are now written against this framework from day one.
modal.impact: The product now reads as a single coherent voice from first screen to last. The tone guide became the onboarding reference for any new team member touching copy — designers, PMs, and engineers included.

cases.title: Helpdesk Website Revamp
cases.description: Restructuring the information architecture of a support site so merchants find answers before they open a ticket — reducing support load through better content structure.

modal.context: The helpdesk site for TrueProfit had grown organically — articles added whenever a question came in, categories created ad hoc, navigation built around internal logic rather than merchant mental models. Merchants were opening support tickets for questions that already had answers, because they couldn't find them.
modal.problem: A support site is only useful if the person looking for help can navigate it without already knowing the answer. The existing structure reflected how the team thought about the product — not how merchants thought about their problems.
modal.approach: I ran a full content audit: every article, every category, every navigation label. I mapped the support tickets coming in against the articles that existed to surface the gaps and the mismatches. Then I used card sorting to understand how merchants grouped information in their own minds. From that research, I restructured the IA around merchant tasks — not product features. Instead of 'Settings > Integrations', the navigation became 'Connect your store' and 'Sync your data.' The language shifted from inside-out to outside-in.
modal.delivered: A revised site map, new navigation taxonomy, rewritten category names and article titles, and a content template for all new helpdesk articles — so future content would be added in a way that fit the structure, not broke it.
modal.impact: Merchants were able to self-serve answers more effectively. The revamp reduced inbound support volume for topics covered by helpdesk articles and became the model for the Ali Reviews support site rebuild.

cases.title: Error Messages That Don't Panic Users
cases.description: Auditing and rewriting the entire error message system for TrueProfit — turning technical failure states into clear, actionable guidance merchants can trust.

modal.context: TrueProfit merchants are tracking their store's profit in real time. When something breaks — a sync fails, data doesn't load, a calculation can't complete — they notice immediately. How we write those moments matters enormously: a bad error message turns a minor technical blip into a trust crisis.
modal.problem: The existing error messages were written by engineers for engineers. They surfaced technical status codes, used passive voice, and gave no direction. Merchants were left wondering: Is my data wrong? Did I do something wrong? Is the app broken? The errors created more questions than they answered.
modal.approach: I audited every error state in the product and categorized them by type: user error, system error, empty state, and connection failure. Each type has a different emotional weight — and needs a different response. A user error needs gentle correction. A system error needs reassurance that it's not the merchant's fault and a clear next step. I developed a writing formula for each error type: What happened → Why it matters → What to do next. Never blame the user. Always give an action. Match the severity of tone to the severity of the problem.
modal.delivered: A rewritten error message library covering all error types, plus a writing formula document for future error states. All new features ship with error copy written to the same standard.
modal.impact: Error-related support tickets dropped after the rewrite. The error message framework was adopted as a standard — any new feature's error states are now written to the formula before shipping.
modal.after: We couldn't sync your store data — this is on our end, not yours. We're already on it. Your existing data is safe. Try refreshing in a few minutes.

cases.title: Design Sprint: Import a CSV File
cases.description: Inside a 5-day design sprint to ship a CSV import feature — how I shaped the UX writing process from problem framing to final copy, under real time pressure.

modal.context: TrueProfit merchants needed a way to import cost data via CSV — a technically straightforward feature with enormous UX complexity. A CSV import flow involves file formatting rules, validation errors, data mapping decisions, and confirmation states. Each step is a potential point of failure and confusion.
modal.problem: The team ran a structured design sprint to go from problem to testable prototype in 5 days. As the UX writer embedded in the sprint, my role was to shape the language at every stage — not just at the end. I was in the room during problem definition, sketching, and prototype building.
modal.approach: A CSV import is a high-stakes interaction. Merchants are touching their cost data — if they make a mistake, their profit numbers break. The copy had to do three things simultaneously: make the steps feel simple, surface errors early and clearly, and give merchants enough confidence to commit. I wrote the full flow copy during the sprint: upload prompt, file validation messages, column mapping instructions, error recovery copy, and the final confirmation state. Every piece was tested with real merchants on Day 4.
modal.delivered: Full UX copy for the CSV import flow — from the upload CTA to every validation and error state — ready to ship at the end of the sprint. Plus a retrospective on how to embed writing earlier in sprint days.
modal.impact: The feature shipped with a copy framework that reduced mapping errors during testing. The sprint process — with a writer embedded from day one — became the team's preferred model for complex feature work.
